§8.5 Applications to Physics

Many problems in physics are solved by calculating integrals.

Work/Energy

If a constant force F F FFF is applied to move an object a distance d d ddd, we define the work W W WWW done to be the product
W = F ⋅ d W = F ⋅ d W=F*dW=F \cdot dW=F⋅d

Units

In metric (SI) units, the unit of force is the Newton, N:
1 N = 1 kg m / s 2 1 N = 1 kg m / s 2 1N=1kgm//s^(2)1 \mathrm{~N}=1 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}1 N=1 kg m/s2
The unit of work/energy is the Joule J:
1 J = 1 N m = 1 kg m 2 / s 2 1 J = 1 N m = 1 kg m 2 / s 2 1J=1Nm=1kgm^(2)//s^(2)1 \mathrm{~J}=1 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}=1 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / \mathrm{s}^{2}1 J=1 N m=1 kg m2/s2
In imperial (English) units, the unit of force is the pound, lb, and the unit of work is the foot-pound, ft lb.
Ex. If it takes 50 lb of force to move a box 10 ft across the floor of my basement, the work I do is
50 × 10 = 500 ft lb . 50 × 10 = 500 ft lb . 50 xx10=500ftlb.50 \times 10=500 \mathrm{ft} \mathrm{lb} .50×10=500ftlb.

Weight

Weight is the force exerted on an object due to the Earth's gravity acting on its mass.
In metric units, the weight of an object of mass m kg m kg mkgm \mathrm{~kg}m kg is

m g m g mgm gmg Newtons

where g = 9.81 m / s 2 g = 9.81 m / s 2 g=9.81m//s^(2)g=9.81 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}g=9.81 m/s2 is the acceleration due to gravity at the Earth's surface.
In English units, the pound is a unit of force (not of mass), so when we say an object is 10 lb , we mean that the weight is 10 lb , i.e., the Earth's gravitational field exerts 10 lb of force on it (n.b. one does not often talk of mass in English units - can you think why this might be so?).
A good example of the difference between mass and weight is to imagine standing on the surface of the moon whose gravity is six times weaker than Earth's. Your weight is 1 6 1 6 (1)/(6)\frac{1}{6}16 that of your weight on Earth, although your mass is unchanged.
Ex. I lift a 2 kg book from the floor onto a shelf 2 m above the floor. The amount of work I do is
2 kg × 9.8 m / s 2 × 2 m = 38.4 J . 2 kg × 9.8 m / s 2 × 2 m = 38.4 J . 2kgxx9.8m//s^(2)xx2m=38.4J.2 \mathrm{~kg} \times 9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2} \times 2 \mathrm{~m}=38.4 \mathrm{~J} .2 kg×9.8 m/s2×2 m=38.4 J.

Work against a variable force

If the force varies with the distance x x xxx, the expression
W = F ⋅ d W = F ⋅ d W=F*dW=F \cdot dW=F⋅d
is replaced with the integral
W = ∫ a b F ( x ) d x W = ∫ a b   F ( x ) d x W=int_(a)^(b)F(x)dxW=\int_{a}^{b} F(x) d xW=∫abF(x)dx
This measures the work done using a variable force F ( x ) F ( x ) F(x)F(x)F(x) to move the object (in a straight line) from x = a x = a x=ax=ax=a to x = b x = b x=bx=bx=b.

Ex. Hooke's Law

If a spring has natural length l l lll and we extend it to a length l ′ l ′ l^(')l^{\prime}l′, then the force exerted by the spring (i.e. the tension) is given by
F = k ( l ′ − l ) F = k l ′ − l F=k(l^(')-l)F=k\left(l^{\prime}-l\right)F=k(l′−l)
where k k kkk is a constant of proportionality called the spring constant. The bigger k k kkk is, the stiffer the spring.
e.g. Suppose we have a spring of natural length 1 m and spring constant 4, which we then extend to 2 m .
Place the spring on the x x xxx-axis so that in the unextended position, the right end of the spring sits at x = 0 x = 0 x=0x=0x=0.

Unextended

Extended
The force exerted when the right end of the spring sits at x ( 0 < x ) x ( 0 < x ) x(0 < x)x(0<x)x(0<x) is then
k ( l ′ − l ) = k ( 1 + x − 1 ) = k x = 4 x as k = 4 k l ′ − l = k ( 1 + x − 1 ) = k x = 4 x  as  k = 4 {:[k(l^(')-l)=k(1+x-1)],[=kx],[=4x quad" as "k=4]:}\begin{aligned} k\left(l^{\prime}-l\right) & =k(1+x-1) \\ & =k x \\ & =4 x \quad \text { as } k=4 \end{aligned}k(l′−l)=k(1+x−1)=kx=4x as k=4
The work done is then
W = ∫ a b F ( x ) d x = ∫ 0 1 4 x d x = [ 2 x 2 ] 0 1 = 2 J W = ∫ a b   F ( x ) d x = ∫ 0 1   4 x d x = 2 x 2 0 1 = 2 J W=int_(a)^(b)F(x)dx=int_(0)^(1)4xdx=[2x^(2)]_(0)^(1)=2JW=\int_{a}^{b} F(x) d x=\int_{0}^{1} 4 x d x=\left[2 x^{2}\right]_{0}^{1}=2 \mathrm{~J}W=∫abF(x)dx=∫014xdx=[2x2]01=2 J

Ex. Chain lifting problem

A 28 m uniform chain with a linear density λ λ lambda\lambdaλ of 2 kg / m 2 kg / m 2kg//m2 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}2 kg/m is dangling from the roof of a building. How much work is needed to pull the chain up onto the top of the building?
Let l l lll represent the amount of chain already hauled up.
At this point 28 − l 28 − l 28-l28-l28−l metres are left to haul up and this amount of chain has mass
2 kg / m × ( 28 − l ) m = ( 56 − 2 l ) kg , 2 kg / m × ( 28 − l ) m = ( 56 − 2 l ) kg , 2kg//mxx(28-l)m=(56-2l)kg,2 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m} \times(28-l) \mathrm{m}=(56-2 l) \mathrm{kg},2 kg/m×(28−l)m=(56−2l)kg,
and weight
( 56 − 2 l ) ⋅ 9.8 ( 56 − 2 l ) ⋅ 9.8 (56-2l)*9.8(56-2 l) \cdot 9.8(56−2l)⋅9.8 Newtons.
The work done to haul this piece of chain up a short distance Δ l Δ l Delta l\Delta lΔl is then approximately the weight × Δ l × Δ l xx Delta l\times \Delta l×Δl, i.e.
Δ W ≈ ( 56 − 2 l ) ⋅ 9.8 Δ l J . Δ W ≈ ( 56 − 2 l ) ⋅ 9.8 Δ l J . Delta W~~(56-2l)*9.8 Delta lJ.\Delta W \approx(56-2 l) \cdot 9.8 \Delta l \mathrm{~J} .ΔW≈(56−2l)⋅9.8Δl J.
Now add all these contributions Δ W Δ W Delta W\Delta WΔW to get
W ≈ ∑ ( 56 − 2 l ) ( 9.8 ) Δ l . W ≈ ∑ ( 56 − 2 l ) ( 9.8 ) Δ l . W~~sum(56-2l)(9.8)Delta l.W \approx \sum(56-2 l)(9.8) \Delta l .W≈∑(56−2l)(9.8)Δl.
As Δ l → 0 Δ l → 0 Delta l rarr0\Delta l \rightarrow 0Δl→0, this Riemann sum becomes an integral and we have
W = ∫ 0 28 ( 56 − 2 l ) ( 9.8 ) d l = 9.8 [ 56 l − l 2 ] 0 28 = 7683.2 J W = ∫ 0 28   ( 56 − 2 l ) ( 9.8 ) d l = 9.8 56 l − l 2 0 28 = 7683.2 J {:[W=int_(0)^(28)(56-2l)(9.8)dl],[=9.8[56 l-l^(2)]_(0)^(28)],[=7683.2J]:}\begin{aligned} W & =\int_{0}^{28}(56-2 l)(9.8) d l \\ & =9.8\left[56 l-l^{2}\right]_{0}^{28} \\ & =7683.2 \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}W=∫028(56−2l)(9.8)dl=9.8[56l−l2]028=7683.2 J
N.B. The book solves this differently by first splitting up the chain and then calculating the work needed to raise each small piece to the top.

Ex. The Space Elevator

A space elevator connects a point on the equator with geostationary orbit, which is approximately 36 , 000 km 36 , 000 km 36,000km36,000 \mathrm{~km}36,000 km above that point.
Calculate the amount of work the elevator has to do in order to lift a 70 kg person from the equator to geostationary orbit. If the elevator is powered by electricity which costs 10 ¢ / kWh 10 ¢ / kWh 10¢//kWh10 ¢ / \mathrm{kWh}10¢/kWh, how much does this cost?
The gravitational attraction (i.e. weight) of the person is given by
G M e ⋅ 70 r 2 ≈ 2.79 × 10 16 r 2 N G M e ⋅ 70 r 2 ≈ 2.79 × 10 16 r 2 N (GM_(e)*70)/(r^(2))~~(2.79 xx10^(16))/(r^(2))N\frac{G M_{e} \cdot 70}{r^{2}} \approx \frac{2.79 \times 10^{16}}{r^{2}} \mathrm{~N}GMe⋅70r2≈2.79×1016r2 N
where G = 6.674 × 10 − 11 Nm 2 / kg 2 G = 6.674 × 10 − 11 Nm 2 / kg 2 G=6.674 xx10^(-11)Nm^(2)//kg^(2)G=6.674 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} / \mathrm{kg}^{2}G=6.674×10−11Nm2/kg2 is the gravitational constant, M e = 5.97 × 10 24 kg M e = 5.97 × 10 24 kg M_(e)=5.97 xx10^(24)kgM_{e}=5.97 \times 10^{24} \mathrm{~kg}Me=5.97×1024 kg is the mass of the Earth, and r r rrr is the distance from the person to the centre of the Earth.
The work required to move the person a small distance Δ r Δ r Delta r\Delta rΔr is then approximately
Δ W ≈ force × distance = 2.79 × 10 16 r 2 Δ r Δ W ≈  force × distance  = 2.79 × 10 16 r 2 Δ r Delta W~~" force × distance "=(2.79 xx10^(16))/(r^(2))Delta r\Delta W \approx \text { force × distance }=\frac{2.79 \times 10^{16}}{r^{2}} \Delta rΔW≈ force × distance =2.79×1016r2Δr
and the total work is then approximately
∑ Δ W = ∑ 2.79 × 10 16 r 2 Δ r ∑ Δ W = ∑ 2.79 × 10 16 r 2 Δ r sum Delta W=sum(2.79 xx10^(16))/(r^(2))Delta r\sum \Delta W=\sum \frac{2.79 \times 10^{16}}{r^{2}} \Delta r∑ΔW=∑2.79×1016r2Δr
As the person travels on the elevator, r r rrr goes from about 6000 km to about 36 , 000 + 6000 = 42 , 000 km 36 , 000 + 6000 = 42 , 000 km 36,000+6000=42,000km36,000+6000=42,000 \mathrm{~km}36,000+6000=42,000 km, i.e. from 6 × 10 6 m 6 × 10 6 m 6xx10^(6)m6 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~m}6×106 m to 4.2 × 10 7 m 4.2 × 10 7 m 4.2 xx10^(7)m4.2 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{~m}4.2×107 m.
Thus as Δ r → 0 Δ r → 0 Delta r rarr0\Delta r \rightarrow 0Δr→0, we get
W = ∫ 6 × 10 6 4.2 × 10 7 2.79 × 10 16 r 2 d r = 2.79 × 10 16 ∫ 6 × 10 6 4.2 × 10 7 1 r 2 d r = 2.79 × 10 16 [ − 1 r ] 6 × 10 6 4.2 × 10 7 ≈ 3.98 × 10 9 J W = ∫ 6 × 10 6 4.2 × 10 7   2.79 × 10 16 r 2 d r = 2.79 × 10 16 ∫ 6 × 10 6 4.2 × 10 7   1 r 2 d r = 2.79 × 10 16 − 1 r 6 × 10 6 4.2 × 10 7 ≈ 3.98 × 10 9 J {:[W=int_(6xx10^(6))^(4.2 xx10^(7))(2.79 xx10^(16))/(r^(2))dr],[=2.79 xx10^(16)int_(6xx10^(6))^(4.2 xx10^(7))(1)/(r^(2))dr],[=2.79 xx10^(16)[-(1)/(r)]_(6xx10^(6))^(4.2 xx10^(7))],[~~3.98 xx10^(9)J]:}\begin{aligned} W & =\int_{6 \times 10^{6}}^{4.2 \times 10^{7}} \frac{2.79 \times 10^{16}}{r^{2}} d r \\ & =2.79 \times 10^{16} \int_{6 \times 10^{6}}^{4.2 \times 10^{7}} \frac{1}{r^{2}} d r \\ & =2.79 \times 10^{16}\left[-\frac{1}{r}\right]_{6 \times 10^{6}}^{4.2 \times 10^{7}} \\ & \approx 3.98 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}W=∫6×1064.2×1072.79×1016r2dr=2.79×1016∫6×1064.2×1071r2dr=2.79×1016[−1r]6×1064.2×107≈3.98×109 J
Now the unit of power in the metric system is the Watt, W, where
1 W = 1 J / s 1 W = 1 J / s 1W=1J//s1 \mathrm{~W}=1 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{s}1 W=1 J/s
1 kWh = 1000 W for 3600 s = 3.6 × 10 6 J . 1 kWh = 1000 W  for  3600 s = 3.6 × 10 6 J . 1kWh=1000W" for "3600s=3.6 xx10^(6)J.1 \mathrm{kWh}=1000 \mathrm{~W} \text { for } 3600 \mathrm{~s}=3.6 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~J} .1kWh=1000 W for 3600 s=3.6×106 J.
Thus the amount of energy required in kWh is
3.98 × 10 9 3.6 × 10 6 ≈ 1107 kWh . 3.98 × 10 9 3.6 × 10 6 ≈ 1107 kWh . (3.98 xx10^(9))/(3.6 xx10^(6))~~1107kWh.\frac{3.98 \times 10^{9}}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} \approx 1107 \mathrm{kWh} .3.98×1093.6×106≈1107kWh.
The cost is then
1107 × 10 ¢ ≈ $ 110.66 . 1107 × 10 ¢ ≈ $ 110.66 . 1107 xx10¢~~$110.66.1107 \times 10 ¢ \approx \$ 110.66 .1107×10¢≈$110.66.
Note that we have neglected factors such as the rotation of the Earth, air resistance, friction and the efficiency of the elevator, not to mention the cost of building it!
Q. So why does it cost about $ 10 , 000 / lb $ 10 , 000 / lb $10,000//lb\$ 10,000 / \mathrm{lb}$10,000/lb to send things into LEO today?

Ex. Conical tank

A conical tank as shown is filled with water from its base. If the density of water is 1000 kg / m 3 1000 kg / m 3 1000kg//m^(3)1000 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}1000 kg/m3, find the amount of work required to completely fill the tank.
Let h h hhh be the depth of water in the tank. The top of the water is approximately a cylinder of radius r r rrr and thickness Δ h Δ h Delta h\Delta hΔh, and the volume of this piece of water is then approximately
π r 2 Δ h . π r 2 Δ h . pir^(2)Delta h.\pi r^{2} \Delta h .πr2Δh.
By similar triangles,
r h = 5 10 = 1 2 , r h = 5 10 = 1 2 , (r)/(h)=(5)/(10)=(1)/(2),\frac{r}{h}=\frac{5}{10}=\frac{1}{2},rh=510=12,
so
r = h 2 . r = h 2 . r=(h)/(2).r=\frac{h}{2} .r=h2.
Thus the volume of this slice of water is approximately
π ( h 2 ) 2 Δ h = π h 2 4 Δ h . π h 2 2 Δ h = π h 2 4 Δ h . pi((h)/(2))^(2)Delta h=(pih^(2))/(4)Delta h.\pi\left(\frac{h}{2}\right)^{2} \Delta h=\frac{\pi h^{2}}{4} \Delta h .π(h2)2Δh=πh24Δh.
The mass is then approximately
1000 ⋅ π h 2 4 Δ h = 250 π h 2 Δ h 1000 ⋅ π h 2 4 Δ h = 250 π h 2 Δ h 1000*(pih^(2))/(4)Delta h=250 pih^(2)Delta h1000 \cdot \frac{\pi h^{2}}{4} \Delta h=250 \pi h^{2} \Delta h1000⋅πh24Δh=250πh2Δh
while the weight is
( 9.81 ) ( 250 π h 2 ) Δ h ( 9.81 ) 250 π h 2 Δ h (9.81)(250 pih^(2))Delta h(9.81)\left(250 \pi h^{2}\right) \Delta h(9.81)(250πh2)Δh
To fill the tank, this slice of water needs to be raised from the base of the tank to a height h h hhh, and so the work done is approximately
Δ W = ( 9.81 ) ( 250 π h 2 ) Δ h ⋅ h = ( 9.81 ) ( 250 π h 3 ) Δ h Δ W = ( 9.81 ) 250 π h 2 Δ h ⋅ h = ( 9.81 ) 250 π h 3 Δ h Delta W=(9.81)(250 pih^(2))Delta h*h=(9.81)(250 pih^(3))Delta h\Delta W=(9.81)\left(250 \pi h^{2}\right) \Delta h \cdot h=(9.81)\left(250 \pi h^{3}\right) \Delta hΔW=(9.81)(250πh2)Δh⋅h=(9.81)(250πh3)Δh
Summing up we get
W ≈ ∑ 9.81 ( 250 π h 3 ) Δ h W ≈ ∑ 9.81 250 π h 3 Δ h W~~sum9.81(250 pih^(3))Delta hW \approx \sum 9.81\left(250 \pi h^{3}\right) \Delta hW≈∑9.81(250πh3)Δh
and in the limit as Δ h → 0 Δ h → 0 Delta h rarr0\Delta h \rightarrow 0Δh→0,
W = ∫ 0 10 ( 9.81 ) ( 250 π h 3 ) d h = ( 9.81 ) ( 250 π ) ∫ 0 10 h 3 d h = ( 9.81 ) ( 250 π ) [ h 4 4 ] 0 10 = ( 9.81 ) ( 250 π ) 10 , 000 4 ≈ 1.926 × 10 7 J W = ∫ 0 10   ( 9.81 ) 250 π h 3 d h = ( 9.81 ) ( 250 π ) ∫ 0 10   h 3 d h = ( 9.81 ) ( 250 π ) h 4 4 0 10 = ( 9.81 ) ( 250 π ) 10 , 000 4 ≈ 1.926 × 10 7 J {:[W=int_(0)^(10)(9.81)(250 pih^(3))dh],[=(9.81)(250 pi)int_(0)^(10)h^(3)dh],[=(9.81)(250 pi)[(h^(4))/(4)]_(0)^(10)],[=(9.81)(250 pi)(10,000)/(4)],[~~1.926 xx10^(7)J]:}\begin{aligned} W & =\int_{0}^{10}(9.81)\left(250 \pi h^{3}\right) d h \\ & =(9.81)(250 \pi) \int_{0}^{10} h^{3} d h \\ & =(9.81)(250 \pi)\left[\frac{h^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{10} \\ & =(9.81)(250 \pi) \frac{10,000}{4} \\ & \approx 1.926 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}W=∫010(9.81)(250πh3)dh=(9.81)(250π)∫010h3dh=(9.81)(250π)[h44]010=(9.81)(250π)10,0004≈1.926×107 J
or 19.26 MJ .
A person using a steady 250 W of power to operate a hand pump would take about 21.4 hours to fill this tank.

Force and Pressure

Recall that pressure is force per unit area. If a constant pressure p p ppp acts on a surface of area A A AAA, then the total force on the surface is
F = p ⋅ A F = p ⋅ A F=p*AF=p \cdot AF=p⋅A
If the pressure varies over the surface, we need integration to calculate the total force.
In metric units, the unit of pressure is the Pascal, Pa, where
1 Pa = 1 N / m 2 1 Pa = 1 N / m 2 1Pa=1N//m^(2)1 \mathrm{~Pa}=1 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}1 Pa=1 N/m2
In imperial (English) units, pressure is usually measured in lbs / ft 2 lbs / ft 2 lbs//ft^(2)\mathrm{lbs} / \mathrm{ft}^{2}lbs/ft2 or lbs / in 2 ( psi ) lbs / in 2 ( psi ) lbs//in^(2)(psi)\mathrm{lbs} / \mathrm{in}^{2}(\mathrm{psi})lbs/in2(psi).
A common example of variable pressure is hydrostatic pressure from being immersed in a liquid such as water.
Hydrostatic pressure acts equally in all directions and at a depth h h hhh below the surface the pressure is given by
p = ρ g h p = ρ g h p=rho ghp=\rho g hp=ρgh
where ρ ρ rho\rhoρ is the density of the fluid and g g ggg is the acceleration due to gravity.
For water,
p = 1000 ( 9.8 ) h Pa p = 1000 ( 9.8 ) h Pa p=1000(9.8)hPap=1000(9.8) h \mathrm{~Pa}p=1000(9.8)h Pa
in metric units, as the density of water is 1000 kg / m 3 1000 kg / m 3 1000kg//m^(3)1000 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}1000 kg/m3. In imperial units, ρ = 62.4 lbs / ft 3 ρ = 62.4 lbs / ft 3 rho=62.4lbs//ft^(3)\rho=62.4 \mathrm{lbs} / \mathrm{ft}^{3}ρ=62.4lbs/ft3 and
p = ( 62.4 ) h lbs / ft 2 p = ( 62.4 ) h lbs / ft 2 p=(62.4)hlbs//ft^(2)p=(62.4) h \mathrm{lbs} / \mathrm{ft}^{2}p=(62.4)hlbs/ft2
Note that in this case, we don't need to multiply by g ( = 32 ft / s 2 ) g = 32 ft / s 2 g(=32ft//s^(2))g\left(=32 \mathrm{ft} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)g(=32ft/s2), as this has already been done.

Ex. Titanic plate

Calculate the hydrostatic force on one side of a plate of the Titanic at the bottom of the ocean if the ocean is 12 , 500 ft 12 , 500 ft 12,500ft12,500 \mathrm{ft}12,500ft deep and the plate is a square 100 ft on a side when
(a) the plate is lying horizontally,
(b) the plate is standing vertically.
(a) Since the pressure exerted depends only on the depth, in this case it is constant and the total force is
F = Pressure × Area = ( 62.4 ) ( 12 , 500 ) × ( 100 ) 2 = 7.8 × 10 9 lbs F =  Pressure × Area  = ( 62.4 ) ( 12 , 500 ) × ( 100 ) 2 = 7.8 × 10 9 lbs {:[F=" Pressure × Area "],[=(62.4)(12","500)xx(100)^(2)],[=7.8 xx10^(9)lbs]:}\begin{aligned} F & =\text { Pressure × Area } \\ & =(62.4)(12,500) \times(100)^{2} \\ & =7.8 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{lbs} \end{aligned}F= Pressure × Area =(62.4)(12,500)×(100)2=7.8×109lbs
(b) In this case, we slice the plate horizontally.
On a thin horizontal strip of area 100 Δ h 100 Δ h 100 Delta h100 \Delta h100Δh, the pressure is approximately constant with value (62.4)h, and so the force on the strip is approximately
Δ F = ( ( 62.4 ) h ) ( 100 Δ h ) . Δ F = ( ( 62.4 ) h ) ( 100 Δ h ) . Delta F=((62.4)h)(100 Delta h).\Delta F=((62.4) h)(100 \Delta h) .ΔF=((62.4)h)(100Δh).
Summing up gives an approximation of the total force
F ≈ ∑ ( 62.4 ) h ⋅ 100 Δ h . F ≈ ∑ ( 62.4 ) h ⋅ 100 Δ h . F~~sum(62.4)h*100 Delta h.F \approx \sum(62.4) h \cdot 100 \Delta h .F≈∑(62.4)h⋅100Δh.
As we let Δ h → 0 Δ h → 0 Delta h rarr0\Delta h \rightarrow 0Δh→0, this Riemann sum becomes an integral and
F = ∫ 12 , 400 12 , 500 ( 62.4 ) h ⋅ 100 d h = 6240 ∫ 12 , 400 12 , 500 h d h = 6240 [ h 2 2 ] 12 , 400 12 , 500 = 6240 2 ( ( 12 , 500 ) 2 − ( 12 , 400 ) 2 ) = 7.7 × 10 9 lbs F = ∫ 12 , 400 12 , 500   ( 62.4 ) h ⋅ 100 d h = 6240 ∫ 12 , 400 12 , 500   h d h = 6240 h 2 2 12 , 400 12 , 500 = 6240 2 ( 12 , 500 ) 2 − ( 12 , 400 ) 2 = 7.7 × 10 9 lbs {:[F=int_(12,400)^(12,500)(62.4)h*100 dh],[=6240int_(12,400)^(12,500)hdh],[=6240[(h^(2))/(2)]_(12,400)^(12,500)],[=(6240)/(2)((12,500)^(2)-(12,400)^(2))],[=7.7 xx10^(9)lbs]:}\begin{aligned} F & =\int_{12,400}^{12,500}(62.4) h \cdot 100 d h \\ & =6240 \int_{12,400}^{12,500} h d h \\ & =6240\left[\frac{h^{2}}{2}\right]_{12,400}^{12,500} \\ & =\frac{6240}{2}\left((12,500)^{2}-(12,400)^{2}\right) \\ & =7.7 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{lbs} \end{aligned}F=∫12,40012,500(62.4)h⋅100dh=6240∫12,40012,500hdh=6240[h22]12,40012,500=62402((12,500)2−(12,400)2)=7.7×109lbs
Explain why this answer is slightly smaller than that in (a).

Ex. Trapezoidal dam

A dam similar to the Hoover dam is in the shape of a trapezoid as below.
If the dam is full to the brim, calculate the total force exerted by the water on the dam.
Divide the dam into horizontal strips of width w w www and height Δ h Δ h Delta h\Delta hΔh.
The width w w www depends on the height h h hhh and is clearly a linear function of h h hhh, i.e.
w ( h ) = a h + b w ( h ) = a h + b w(h)=ah+bw(h)=a h+bw(h)=ah+b
Also, when h = 0 , w = 400 h = 0 , w = 400 h=0,w=400h=0, w=400h=0,w=400 and when h = 220 , w = 200 h = 220 , w = 200 h=220,w=200h=220, w=200h=220,w=200. So,
a ( 0 ) + b = 400 a ( 220 ) + b = 200 a ( 0 ) + b = 400 a ( 220 ) + b = 200 {:[a(0)+b=400],[a(220)+b=200]:}\begin{aligned} a(0)+b & =400 \\ a(220)+b & =200 \end{aligned}a(0)+b=400a(220)+b=200
giving b = 400 b = 400 b=400b=400b=400 and
220 a + 400 = 200 ⇒ 220 a = − 200 ⇒ a = − 200 220 = − 10 11 220 a + 400 = 200 ⇒ 220 a = − 200 ⇒ a = − 200 220 = − 10 11 220 a+400=200=>220 a=-200=>a=-(200)/(220)=-(10)/(11)220 a+400=200 \Rightarrow 220 a=-200 \Rightarrow a=-\frac{200}{220}=-\frac{10}{11}220a+400=200⇒220a=−200⇒a=−200220=−1011
Hence
w = 400 − 10 11 h w = 400 − 10 11 h w=400-(10)/(11)hw=400-\frac{10}{11} hw=400−1011h
The area of a small strip is then approximately
( 400 − 10 11 h ) Δ h 400 − 10 11 h Δ h (400-(10)/(11)h)Delta h\left(400-\frac{10}{11} h\right) \Delta h(400−1011h)Δh
while the water pressure on this strip is approximately constant with value
( 1000 ) ( 9.8 ) h ( 1000 ) ( 9.8 ) h (1000)(9.8)h(1000)(9.8) h(1000)(9.8)h
The force on this strip is then approximately
Δ F ≈ ( 1000 ) ( 9.8 ) h ⋅ ( 400 − 10 11 h ) Δ h Δ F ≈ ( 1000 ) ( 9.8 ) h ⋅ 400 − 10 11 h Δ h Delta F~~(1000)(9.8)h*(400-(10)/(11)h)Delta h\Delta F \approx(1000)(9.8) h \cdot\left(400-\frac{10}{11} h\right) \Delta hΔF≈(1000)(9.8)h⋅(400−1011h)Δh
The total force, F F FFF, is then approximately
F ≈ ∑ Δ F = ∑ ( 1000 ) ( 9.8 ) h ( 400 − 10 11 h ) Δ h F ≈ ∑ Δ F = ∑ ( 1000 ) ( 9.8 ) h 400 − 10 11 h Δ h F~~sum Delta F=sum(1000)(9.8)h(400-(10)/(11)h)Delta hF \approx \sum \Delta F=\sum(1000)(9.8) h\left(400-\frac{10}{11} h\right) \Delta hF≈∑ΔF=∑(1000)(9.8)h(400−1011h)Δh
and as Δ h → 0 Δ h → 0 Delta h rarr0\Delta h \rightarrow 0Δh→0 we get
F = ∫ 0 220 ( 1000 ) ( 9.8 ) h ( 400 − 10 11 h ) d h = 9800 ∫ 0 220 ( 400 h − 10 11 h 2 ) d h = 9800 [ 200 h 2 − 10 33 h 3 ] 0 220 ≈ 6.32 × 10 10 N F = ∫ 0 220   ( 1000 ) ( 9.8 ) h 400 − 10 11 h d h = 9800 ∫ 0 220   400 h − 10 11 h 2 d h = 9800 200 h 2 − 10 33 h 3 0 220 ≈ 6.32 × 10 10 N {:[F=int_(0)^(220)(1000)(9.8)h(400-(10)/(11)h)dh],[=9800int_(0)^(220)(400 h-(10)/(11)h^(2))dh],[=9800[200h^(2)-(10)/(33)h^(3)]_(0)^(220)],[~~6.32 xx10^(10)N]:}\begin{aligned} F & =\int_{0}^{220}(1000)(9.8) h\left(400-\frac{10}{11} h\right) d h \\ & =9800 \int_{0}^{220}\left(400 h-\frac{10}{11} h^{2}\right) d h \\ & =9800\left[200 h^{2}-\frac{10}{33} h^{3}\right]_{0}^{220} \\ & \approx 6.32 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned}F=∫0220(1000)(9.8)h(400−1011h)dh=9800∫0220(400h−1011h2)dh=9800[200h2−1033h3]0220≈6.32×1010 N
